content.text: Every Python agent project accumulates a web of dependencies: LLM client SDKs, HTTP libraries, database drivers, observability tools, and evaluation frameworks. Package managers — pip, uv, and Poetry — handle installing, version-resolving, and locking these libraries so that every developer workstation, CI runner, and production container runs identical code. Choosing and using the right tool is foundational to reproducible, deployable agentic systems.

A Python package manager is a program that downloads library distributions from a package index (primarily PyPI), resolves version constraints across all dependencies, and installs the resulting set of packages into a Python environment. The three tools covered here span a spectrum from minimal to full lifecycle management.
is Python's built-in installer, shipping with every CPython release since 3.4. It reads
files (a flat list of package specifiers), fetches matching wheels from PyPI, and extracts them into the active environment. pip alone does not produce a lockfile — you must run
afterwards to capture exact installed versions, but that snapshot includes transitive dependencies mixed with your direct ones.
is a Rust-based resolver and installer released by Astral (the team behind Ruff). It is a drop-in replacement for pip and pip-tools that is 10–100× faster. uv provides
to install exactly those pins. As of mid-2025, uv also manages virtual environments, Python interpreter installations, and project-level workflows via
is a full project lifecycle manager. It reads a single
for both project metadata (name, version, author) and dependency constraints, resolves them, and writes a

file that pins every package at an exact version with a content hash. Poetry also handles publishing to PyPI, managing virtual environments automatically, and separating dependency groups (main, dev, docs, eval).
The correctness of an AI agent can be silently broken by a dependency version bump. The

client library, for example, changed tool-calling response shapes between v1.30 and v1.40, and streaming semantics again in v1.50. If a developer's laptop runs openai 1.50 but a production Docker image installs whatever latest version PyPI serves, the agent can fail in production while all local tests pass.

Beyond correctness, dependency management matters for speed and cost in CI pipelines. An agentic project can easily have 80–120 transitive packages. Without a lockfile and a fast installer, every CI job resolves and downloads gigabytes of packages from scratch. uv cuts a 60-second pip install to under five seconds by parallelizing downloads and caching resolved metadata.
Security is another driver. Each package in a transitive closure is a potential supply-chain attack surface. Tools like

scan installed packages against the OSV (Open Source Vulnerabilities) database. Without a precise list of what is installed — which requires a lockfile — auditing is unreliable: the set you audit is not guaranteed to be the set that runs in production.

content: a lockfile pins every transitive package to an exact version and content hash, so `pip sync` on any machine produces a bit-for-bit identical environment.
Rust-based parallel resolver and HTTP/2 download pipeline makes cold installs 10–100× faster than pip, reducing CI wait time dramatically.
optional dependency groups (dev, eval, docs) keep production images slim by excluding test and linting tools.
pyproject.toml centralizes project name, version, Python constraint, and all dependency groups — no more requirements.txt alongside setup.py alongside setup.cfg.
mounts the local source tree into the environment, so import changes take effect immediately without reinstalling.
Poetry (and uv) can build and upload a package to PyPI in one command, useful for agent frameworks published as libraries.
version-controlled lockfiles make dependency changes visible in pull requests, enabling review and attribution.
running pip freeze without curation records every transitive package as a direct dependency, creating hundreds of pins that are hard to upgrade and obscure what you actually depend on.
Poetry creates and manages its own virtualenv by default, which conflicts with teams that use system venvs or nix shells. Disabling auto-env creation requires extra configuration.
uv's native lock format (uv.lock) is newer than poetry.lock and less integrated with third-party tooling like Dependabot, though support is growing rapidly.
mixing pip, uv, and Poetry in a single repo creates confusion. Teams must standardize on one tool and document it clearly.
on exotic architectures (ARM, Alpine Linux), PyPI may not have pre-built wheels, falling back to slow C-extension compilation — a problem all Python package managers share.
Python package managers do not install system libraries (CUDA, libpq). Those must be handled by Docker base images or Conda.

content.text: When you declare a dependency like

, the package manager must find a version of openai that satisfies that range AND satisfies the ranges that openai itself declares for its own dependencies (httpx, pydantic, etc.), all simultaneously. This is a constraint satisfaction problem (CSP) often modeled as a Boolean satisfiability (SAT) problem. pip uses a backtracking resolver introduced in v20.3. uv uses a custom DPLL-style SAT solver written in Rust that is dramatically faster than pip's Python implementation.

The resolver queries a package index (PyPI by default) for metadata about each candidate version — which Python versions it supports, which extras it provides, and which packages it depends on. This metadata is available from PyPI's JSON API without downloading the full wheel, so good resolvers minimize actual downloads during resolution.

A lockfile records the complete, fully-expanded dependency graph with exact version numbers and content hashes (SHA-256 of the wheel file). When installing from a lockfile, the manager skips resolution entirely — it already knows exactly what to fetch and can verify integrity by checking hashes. This is what makes lockfile-based installs both fast and secure.
Lockfiles are generated from your abstract constraints (

) and must be regenerated when constraints change. The correct workflow is: edit constraints → regenerate lock → commit both files → all other machines install from the lock. Never edit a lockfile by hand.
Python's module system resolves imports by searching directories in
. A virtual environment is a lightweight directory tree with its own

folder, a symlink to the Python interpreter, and scripts that prepend itself to PATH and sys.path when activated. All three tools (pip, uv, Poetry) install packages into an active or specified virtual environment, keeping project dependencies isolated from each other and from the system Python.

The diagram below shows the full dependency lifecycle from declaration to deployment. Every arrow represents a data transformation: constraints become resolved versions, resolved versions become a lockfile, and the lockfile drives both local development and production installs.

attrs.alt: Package management architecture: Declare → Resolve → Lock → Install → Deploy

content.text: The key insight is that the lockfile acts as a firewall between the non-deterministic resolution step (which depends on what PyPI serves at a given moment) and the deterministic install step (which always produces the same environment). Commit the lockfile so that resolution only happens when a developer intentionally changes constraints.
Choose your package manager based on team size, project type, and operational context:

content: small scripts, educational projects, Dockerfile workflows where you want minimal tooling. Acceptable for single-developer projects where pip freeze output is carefully curated.
teams already using requirements.txt who want proper lockfile support without switching to a new tool entirely.

any project prioritizing CI speed. Excellent for monorepos, large dependency graphs, and Docker builds. The drop-in pip compatibility means zero migration risk. Recommended for new agentic AI projects.

when building an agent framework or library that will be published to PyPI. Poetry's build system, version management, and publishing workflow are best-in-class. Also good for teams wanting all configuration in pyproject.toml.
when dependencies include non-Python binary packages (CUDA libraries, compiled ML frameworks). Less common for pure API-calling agents.

content.text: A practical rule of thumb: use uv for applications (agents, services) and Poetry or uv for libraries. Avoid pip alone for anything beyond quick experiments — the lack of a proper lockfile is a reliability liability.

When to avoid virtual environments inside containers: if your deployment target is a Docker container with a fixed base image, the container itself provides isolation. Install directly into the container's system Python with
to avoid the overhead of activating a venv at container startup.
Setting up a new project with uv (recommended)

# 1. Install uv (one-time, no Python needed)
curl -Lsf https://astral.sh/uv/install.sh | sh

# 2. Create a new project directory with pyproject.toml
uv init my-agent --python 3.12
cd my-agent

# 3. Create a virtual environment
uv venv                        # creates .venv using the project Python

# 4. Add dependencies
uv add openai httpx pydantic   # writes to pyproject.toml and resolves immediately

# 5. Add dev-only dependencies
uv add --dev pytest ruff

# 6. Generate a lockfile (uv.lock)
uv lock

# 7. Install everything (for a fresh clone)
uv sync                        # installs from uv.lock, creates .venv if missing

# 8. Run a script inside the project environment
uv run python src/agent.py

# 1. Install Poetry
pip install poetry              # or use the official installer

# 2. Create a new project
poetry new my-agent
cd my-agent

# 3. Add dependencies (updates pyproject.toml + poetry.lock)
poetry add openai httpx pydantic

# 4. Add dev dependencies in a group
poetry add --group dev pytest ruff

# 5. Install the project (all groups)
poetry install

# 6. Install production only (no dev tools)
poetry install --without dev

# 7. Activate the Poetry-managed venv
poetry shell

# 8. Run without activating
poetry run python my_agent/main.py

# requirements.in (direct deps only — human-maintained)
# openai>=1.40,<2
# httpx>=0.27
# pydantic>=2.7

# Compile into a locked requirements.txt
pip install pip-tools
pip-compile requirements.in -o requirements.lock

# Install from the lock
python -m venv .venv
source .venv/bin/activate
pip install -r requirements.lock

# Upgrade and re-lock
pip-compile --upgrade requirements.in -o requirements.lock
This example shows the full setup sequence for a minimal LLM agent using uv, from blank directory to a runnable Python file with pinned dependencies.

# Directory structure after setup
my-agent/
  pyproject.toml
  uv.lock
  .venv/
  src/
    agent.py

# pyproject.toml
[project]
name = "my-agent"
version = "0.1.0"
requires-python = ">=3.12"
dependencies = [
  "openai>=1.40,<2",
  "httpx>=0.27",
  "pydantic>=2.7",
]

[project.optional-dependencies]
dev = ["pytest>=8", "ruff>=0.5"]
eval = ["deepeval>=1.0"]

[build-system]
requires = ["hatchling"]
build-backend = "hatchling.build"

# src/agent.py — runs after: uv sync && uv run python src/agent.py
import openai

client = openai.OpenAI()  # reads OPENAI_API_KEY from environment

def run_agent(user_message: str) -> str:
    response = client.chat.completions.create(
        model="gpt-4o-mini",
        messages=[
            {"role": "system", "content": "You are a helpful assistant."},
            {"role": "user", "content": user_message},
        ],
    )
    return response.choices[0].message.content

if __name__ == "__main__":
    print(run_agent("What is 2 + 2?"))
A production Dockerfile that separates dependency installation from application code for optimal layer caching, using uv for fast installs.

# syntax=docker/dockerfile:1
FROM python:3.12-slim AS builder

# Install uv
RUN pip install uv --no-cache-dir

WORKDIR /app

# Copy only lock and project metadata first (maximizes Docker cache hits)
COPY pyproject.toml uv.lock ./

# Install production deps only into /app/.venv
RUN uv sync --frozen --no-dev --no-install-project

# Now copy application source
COPY src/ ./src/

# Install the project itself (editable or not)
RUN uv sync --frozen --no-dev

# ---- Runtime image ----
FROM python:3.12-slim AS runtime
WORKDIR /app

# Copy the pre-built venv from builder
COPY --from=builder /app/.venv /app/.venv
COPY --from=builder /app/src /app/src

# Add venv to PATH
ENV PATH="/app/.venv/bin:$PATH"

CMD ["python", "src/agent.py"]
A mid-sized agentic platform with four services (orchestrator, tool-executor, embedding-worker, evaluation-runner) uses the following pattern. Each service is a separate directory with its own
via uv's workspace feature. This means resolution happens once across all packages, guaranteeing that the shared internal library (
) uses the same version of openai in all four services.

# uv-workspace/pyproject.toml (workspace root)
[tool.uv.workspace]
members = [
  "services/orchestrator",
  "services/tool-executor",
  "services/embedding-worker",
  "services/eval-runner",
  "libs/agent-core",
]

# services/orchestrator/pyproject.toml
[project]
name = "orchestrator"
requires-python = ">=3.12"
dependencies = [
  "agent-core",          # local workspace member
  "openai>=1.50,<2",
  "fastapi>=0.115",
]

flag refuses to update the lockfile if it is out of sync with pyproject.toml, catching accidental lockfile omissions before they reach production. Total cold-install time for 140 packages (including torch-cpu for the embedding service) is 18 seconds with uv versus 4 minutes 10 seconds with pip.

# WRONG — captures every transitive dep as a direct dep
pip install openai httpx
pip freeze > requirements.txt
# requirements.txt now contains 60+ packages including:
# certifi==2024.2.2
# h11==0.14.0
# sniffio==1.3.1  ...

# RIGHT — only list direct deps in requirements.in, then compile
echo "openai>=1.40,<2" >> requirements.in
echo "httpx>=0.27" >> requirements.in
uv pip compile requirements.in -o requirements.lock
Mistake 2: unpinned LLM SDK in production

# WRONG — allows any future version
dependencies = ["openai"]

# WRONG — too broad; a major bump can break tool calling
dependencies = ["openai>=1"]

# RIGHT — pin to a compatible minor range and re-test before widening
dependencies = ["openai>=1.50,<1.60"]
Mistake 3: committing uv.lock but not pyproject.toml
The lockfile must always be committed alongside its source of constraints. A lockfile without a
is meaningless because there is no way to know what direct dependencies were intended. Both files belong in version control.

# WRONG — pollutes system Python, causes version conflicts between projects
sudo pip install openai

# RIGHT — always activate or specify a venv first
python -m venv .venv && source .venv/bin/activate
pip install openai

# or with uv (automatically uses project venv)
uv add openai
Mistake 5: mixing Poetry and pip in the same project

inside a Poetry-managed project bypasses Poetry's resolver and can create an inconsistent state where poetry.lock does not reflect what is actually installed. Always use the tool that owns the environment:

content: choose pip-tools, uv, or Poetry and use it everywhere. Document the choice in README and enforce it in CI with a check that fails if lockfile and pyproject.toml diverge.
uv.lock, poetry.lock, and requirements.lock all belong in version control. Excluding them defeats the reproducibility guarantee.
or
prevents CI from silently updating the lock on every run.
keep test, linting, eval, and documentation dependencies in separate groups. Production Docker images install only the default group; CI installs all groups.

content.text: Bound upper versions on LLM SDKs:

content: major version bumps of openai and anthropic change API shapes. Pin to a minor range and bump intentionally after testing.

content.text: Use editable installs for local packages:

content: or
(uv installs local packages as editable by default) lets you edit source code without reinstalling.
Docker layer caching on pyproject.toml + lockfile means the dependency install layer is only rebuilt when deps change, not on every code commit.
diff the lockfile in pull requests. An unexpected new package or version bump in a lockfile diff is a signal worth investigating before merging.

content.text: Package installation time is a real cost in CI/CD pipelines that run on every push. For a typical agentic project with 80–120 transitive packages, benchmark results show the following cold-install times (no cache, fresh virtualenv):

uv's speed advantage comes from three sources: the resolver is written in Rust and uses a DPLL-style SAT solver; downloads are parallelized over HTTP/2; and wheels are extracted in parallel using multi-threading. For Docker builds, uv also supports a global wheel cache mounted via BuildKit's cache mounts, reducing downloads to near zero on repeated builds.

# Using BuildKit cache mount for uv wheel cache
FROM python:3.12-slim
RUN pip install uv
COPY pyproject.toml uv.lock ./
RUN --mount=type=cache,target=/root/.cache/uv \
    uv sync --frozen --no-dev

For monorepos with many services, uv workspaces share a single resolution across all packages, avoiding redundant resolution and ensuring version consistency. Poetry's workspace support (via path dependencies) achieves similar consistency but resolves each package independently unless you use the
The Python packaging ecosystem extends beyond the core installers:

content: scans installed packages against OSV (Open Source Vulnerabilities) and GitHub Advisory databases. Run in CI:
the classic pip-compile / pip-sync pair for teams already comfortable with requirements.txt but wanting lockfile semantics.
GitHub bots that open pull requests when dependencies have updates or CVEs. Both support pyproject.toml and lockfiles.
the public package index at pypi.org. Supports JSON metadata API for fast resolver queries and simple repository protocol (PEP 503).
Nexus Repository, Artifactory, AWS CodeArtifact, and Google Artifact Registry can host private Python packages. Configure with
in pyproject.toml or
linter and formatter often installed as a dev dependency, enforces import ordering (isort-compatible) and code style.
another PEP 517 build backend and project manager that integrates well with uv for building and publishing packages.

content.text:
# Configuring a private index alongside PyPI (uv)
[[tool.uv.index]]
name = "private"
url = "https://nexus.example.com/repository/pypi/simple"
priority = "supplemental"   # check PyPI first, private for unlisted packages

# For packages that exist only on the private index:
[[tool.uv.index]]
name = "internal"
url = "https://nexus.example.com/repository/pypi-internal/simple"
priority = "primary"

For agentic AI projects in 2025, uv is the pragmatic default for applications and services. Poetry remains the go-to for library authors. pip + pip-tools suits teams with existing infrastructure that want to migrate incrementally.
The Python package ecosystem has been a target for supply-chain attacks (typosquatting, malicious sdist build scripts, dependency confusion). Package managers provide several layers of defense:

content: lockfiles record the SHA-256 hash of every wheel. uv and pip --require-hashes verify each download matches before installing, preventing man-in-the-middle substitution even if PyPI is compromised.
when using private registries, configure the index priority so private packages cannot be shadowed by malicious PyPI packages with the same name. Use
for packages that should only come from a specific index.
run pip-audit on every PR to catch newly disclosed CVEs in the dependency tree before they reach production.
install only the non-dev dependency group in production Docker images. Dev and eval packages often pull in tools with larger attack surfaces.
verify that the lockfile matches pyproject.toml in CI using
or
. A lockfile that diverges from declared constraints is a reliability risk.

content.text:
# In CI pipeline: fail if lockfile is out of sync
uv lock --check

# Verify hashes during install (uv does this by default from uv.lock)
uv sync --frozen

# Audit for CVEs
pip install pip-audit
pip-audit -r <(uv pip freeze) --format json --output audit-report.json

# Check for dependency confusion with explicit index assignment
# In pyproject.toml:
# [[tool.uv.index]]
# name = "internal"
# url = "https://nexus.example.com/pypi/simple"
# priority = "explicit"   # packages with explicit = true ONLY come from this index

Reliability under operational conditions means ensuring that your CI and deployment pipelines can install dependencies even when PyPI is partially unavailable. Operate a private mirror (Nexus, Artifactory) that proxies and caches PyPI. All installs go through the mirror: if PyPI is down, the mirror serves cached wheels. Configure uv or pip with the mirror URL as the primary index.

content: installs packages from PyPI; always activate or specify a virtual environment to avoid polluting the system Python.
is the fastest Python installer/resolver (Rust-based, 10–100× faster than pip) and the recommended default for new agentic AI projects.
provides a complete lifecycle (resolve, lock, build, publish) and is the best choice for agent frameworks that are published as libraries.

content.text: Always commit the lockfile (uv.lock, poetry.lock, requirements.lock) alongside pyproject.toml — without it, environments are non-reproducible.
Declare only direct dependencies in pyproject.toml; never rely on pip freeze output as a hand-maintained requirements file.
Separate optional dependency groups (dev, eval, docs) to keep production Docker images slim and reduce the security attack surface.
Bound LLM SDK versions to a compatible minor range and bump intentionally after testing — silent API changes in openai or anthropic can break agent tool-calling logic.
Run pip-audit in CI and use lockfile hash verification to defend against supply-chain attacks and newly disclosed CVEs.
Cache the lockfile-driven install layer in Docker and CI: copy pyproject.toml + lockfile first, run uv sync, then copy application source.
